questions.question: Who was the first man to run a sub-4-minute mile?
questions.explanation: Roger Bannister became the first person to run a mile in under four minutes on May 6, 1954, at Iffley Road Track in Oxford, with a time of 3:59.4. His record was broken just 46 days later by Australian John Landy. Bannister was also a neurologist and later became Master of Pembroke College, Oxford.
questions.memory_hook: Bannister broke the 4-minute mile — then Landy broke his record 46 days later.

questions.question: The 'Protestant Reformation' was triggered by Martin Luther nailing his 95 Theses to a church door in which year?
questions.explanation: Martin Luther nailed his 95 Theses to the door of the Castle Church in Wittenberg on October 31, 1517 (now celebrated as Reformation Day). The theses challenged Catholic Church practices, particularly the sale of indulgences. The printing press allowed Luther's ideas to spread rapidly across Europe.
questions.memory_hook: 1517: Luther nailed it. 95 theses, one door, one revolution.

questions.question: What is the maximum number of clubs a golfer can carry in their bag?
questions.explanation: A golfer may carry a maximum of 14 clubs in their bag during a round, as per the Rules of Golf established by the USGA and R&A. Most professionals carry: driver, 3-wood, 5-wood, 4-iron through pitching wedge, sand wedge, lob wedge, and putter — typically 14.
questions.memory_hook: 14 clubs maximum — one for each type of shot, roughly.

questions.question: Which swimmer won 8 gold medals at the 2008 Beijing Olympics — the most by any athlete at a single Games — breaking Mark Spitz's 1972 record of 7?
questions.explanation: Michael Phelps won 8 gold medals at the 2008 Beijing Olympics, surpassing Mark Spitz's 1972 record of 7. Phelps went on to become the most decorated Olympian of all time with 23 gold medals and 28 total medals across four Olympic Games.
questions.memory_hook: Phelps 2008 Beijing = 8 golds. Most decorated Olympian ever.

questions.question: Which band wrote and performed 'Comfortably Numb'?
questions.explanation: 'Comfortably Numb' appears on Pink Floyd's concept album 'The Wall' (1979). The song features two solos by guitarist David Gilmour, with the outro solo widely considered one of the greatest guitar solos of all time. Roger Waters wrote the lyrics; Gilmour wrote the music.
questions.memory_hook: 'Comfortably Numb' — Pink Floyd's Wall, Gilmour's guitar solo, Waters' words.

questions.question: Which 1999 science fiction film starring Keanu Reeves depicts a dystopian future where humans live in a simulated reality controlled by machines?
questions.explanation: The Matrix (1999), directed by the Wachowskis, became a landmark in science fiction cinema. It popularized 'bullet time' photography, grossed over $460 million worldwide, and spawned a franchise while introducing philosophical concepts about simulated reality to mainstream audiences.
questions.memory_hook: The Matrix (1999) = red pill or blue pill. 'What is real?'

questions.question: What was the name of Whitney Houston's character in 'The Bodyguard'?
questions.explanation: Whitney Houston played Rachel Marron, a pop star under threat from a stalker, in The Bodyguard (1992). Kevin Costner played her bodyguard Frank Farmer. The film's soundtrack became one of the best-selling soundtracks of all time, largely on the strength of Houston's 'I Will Always Love You.'
questions.memory_hook: Rachel Marron was Whitney's character — a pop star who needed protecting, like Whitney herself.

questions.question: The 'Battle of Thermopylae' is famous for which force's last stand?
questions.explanation: The Battle of Thermopylae (480 BC) saw approximately 300 Spartans (and about 700 Thespians) under King Leonidas hold off a Persian army estimated in the hundreds of thousands for three days. Their sacrifice allowed the Greek forces to regroup and eventually defeat the Persians at Salamis.
questions.memory_hook: 300 Spartans vs. Persia — 'Tonight we dine in hell!' Three days, one narrow pass.

questions.question: Indonesia has the most islands of any country. Approximately how many does it have?
questions.explanation: Indonesia consists of approximately 17,000 islands, of which about 6,000 are inhabited. It is the world's largest archipelago nation. Major islands include Java, Sumatra, Borneo (shared with Malaysia and Brunei), Sulawesi, and half of New Guinea. With 277 million people, it is the world's fourth most populous country.
questions.memory_hook: Indonesia = 17,000 islands. About 6,000 inhabited. It's the world's largest archipelago — imagine running a country spread across the ocean over an area wider than the continental US.

questions.question: What is the name of the layer of rock that makes up Earth's outer surface?
questions.explanation: Earth's crust is the thin, solid outer shell of the planet. Continental crust is about 35-70 km thick; oceanic crust is only about 5-10 km thick. Below the crust is the mantle, a thick layer of semi-solid rock. At the center is the core, divided into a liquid outer core and solid inner core.
questions.memory_hook: Earth's layers: crust, mantle, outer core, inner core. The crust is like the skin of an apple — relatively thin compared to the whole. You live on the thinnest possible layer above an ocean of hot rock.

questions.question: The Atlas Mountains, a range spanning approximately 2,500 km, run across which three North African countries?
questions.explanation: The Atlas Mountains run across Morocco, Algeria, and Tunisia from the Atlantic coast near Agadir to northeastern Tunisia. Toubkal in Morocco (4,167 m) is the highest peak. The Atlas range forms a geographic barrier between the Mediterranean and Atlantic coasts (relatively fertile) and the Sahara Desert to the south. The Berber (Amazigh) people have inhabited the Atlas for thousands of years. The mountains are named after the Titan Atlas from Greek mythology.
questions.memory_hook: Atlas Mountains: Morocco + Algeria + Tunisia, ~2,500 km. Toubkal (4,167m) = highest peak. Separates Mediterranean coast from Sahara. Home of Berber/Amazigh people for millennia. Named after the Greek Titan Atlas, who holds up the sky — fitting for a range that holds back the desert.

questions.question: What is the term for different forms of the same chemical element that have the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons in their nuclei?
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element that have different numbers of neutrons, giving them different atomic masses. Carbon-12 (6 protons, 6 neutrons) and carbon-14 (6 protons, 8 neutrons) are isotopes of carbon. Most isotopes are stable, but some are radioactive — their nuclei decay over time. Carbon-14's radioactive decay is used in radiocarbon dating. Uranium-235 and uranium-238 are isotopes; U-235 is fissile (used in reactors and bombs) while U-238 is not.
questions.memory_hook: Isotopes = same element, different neutron counts, different mass. Carbon-12 vs Carbon-14 — same chemistry, but C-14 is radioactive. C-14 dating works because C-14's half-life (5,730 years) is predictable. U-235 is fissile (used in reactors); U-238 is not. Same element, completely different behavior.
